c1.texto_completo:
Pre Professional Practice Report 
Facultad de Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas – Universidad de los Andes 
Tomás Lehmann 
Ingeniería Civil Industrial 
Smart Dock, Soluciones Náuticas 
 Design, manufacturing, and installation of modular docks and hydraulic lifts for watercraft.
 
Abstract 
Smart Dock is a company dedicated to the design and production of modular docks and 
hydraulic lifts for boats and jet skis. During my pre-professional practice, I participated in 
activities related to material logistics for the installation of docks and lifts, technical and 
economic project analysis, and direct interaction with clients. Among the most relevant 
tasks were the planning and coordination of modular structure installations. As part of this 
planning process, we requested photographs from clients showing the desired installation 
area, along with information regarding lakebed conditions, in order to evaluate alternative 
assembly and installation solutions. 
 
The main challenge consisted of integrating technical and logistical criteria in projects with 
time and environmental constraints, where installation planning was critical. This was 
particularly relevant given that docks and hydraulic lifts were installed across several lakes 
in southern Chile, while construction and installation materials were stored in Cunco, 
Araucanía. Each project had to be completed within a single day, and for jet ski lifts it was 
possible to complete two different projects in one day. 
 

The results of my internship were reflected in the significant development of my skills in 
operational management, engineering analysis, and professional communication, 
strengthening my training and my ability to adapt to multidisciplinary environments.
 
1. Introduction 
1.1 Company Description 
Smart Dock is a Chilean company dedicated to the design, manufacturing, and installation 
of nautical infrastructure solutions, specializing in modular docks and hydraulic lifts for 
boats and jet skis. The company began its operations in 2019, initially focusing on the 
installation of hydraulic lift systems. Figure 1 presents an example of a hydraulic lift 
system installed by the company. 
 
Figure 1. Hydraulic lift system for boats and jet skis installed by Smart Dock. 
Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). 

Starting in 2020, Smart Dock incorporated modular dock systems. These systems have been 
widely implemented in lakes across southern Chile, as illustrated in Figure 2, enabling 
Smart Dock to reduce installation times and operate efficiently. 
 
 
Figure 2. Modular dock system installed in a lake in southern Chile. 
Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). 
Since its foundation, the company has developed more than 200 projects across various 
lakes in southern Chile. On average, Smart Dock executes more than 40 projects per year, 
maintaining a high project turnover and an operational capacity that allows it to manage 
multiple installations simultaneously. Based on the scale and volume of its operations, the 
company generates estimated annual revenues of 400 million Chilean pesos, demonstrating 
sustainable growth and operational stability. 
Organizational Chart 
 
The company has approximately 20 employees and operates under an integrated model that 
combines engineering, manufacturing and installation. This structure, which facilitates 
coordination and decision-making, is presented in Figure 3. 

 
 
Figure 3. Organizational chart of Smart Dock. 
Source: Own elaboration. 
1.2 Department / Area Description 
My professional internship was carried out mainly within the Project Execution area, which 
is responsible for planning, coordinating, and executing the installation of modular docks 
and hydraulic lift systems. This area plays a key role in connecting engineering design, 
production, logistics, and on-site installation activities. 
 
The main responsibilities of the Operations and Project Execution area include material 
planning for each project, coordination with the production team, scheduling of 
installations, and supervision of on-site execution. Due to the modular and prefabricated 
nature of the company’s solutions, the area must ensure that all components are properly 
prepared prior to transportation in order to meet strict installation timelines. 
 
Within this area, I worked directly under the supervision of the Project Manager – Field 
Operations, maintaining close interaction with the production and logistics teams. My 
responsibilities included supporting the planning and preparation of materials, coordinating 

with the production team to align fabrication and delivery schedules, and assisting in on-
site installation activities. I also participated in the technical and economic evaluation of 
projects and supported communication with clients to gather relevant information for 
installation planning. 
 
The Operations and Project Execution area maintains constant interaction with other 
departments, particularly Production and Supply Chain, to ensure material availability and 
quality. This coordination is essential for completing projects efficiently, often within a 
single day, and for adapting installation strategies to site-specific conditions. 
 
Overall, this area provided a multidisciplinary working environment that allowed me to 
apply engineering concepts in real projects while developing operational, organizational, 
and communication skills. My supervisor was Agustín Molina García, who was responsible 
for coordinating projects from their design phase through to installation. The working 
relationship was close and focused on continuous feedback, which allowed me to develop 
both technical and operational criteria.
 
2. Work Details 
2.1 General Activities 
During the internship period, I carried out various activities aimed at supporting the 
execution of Smart Dock’s nautical infrastructure projects, with an emphasis on logistics 
planning, internal coordination, and on-site installation support. 
 
One of my main responsibilities was the planning and preparation of the materials required 
for each project, considering the technical specifications and the specific configuration of 

each modular dock or hydraulic lift system. This planning involved defining in advance the 
quantity and type of modules, metal structures, and auxiliary components required, with the 
objective of ensuring continuity during installation and avoiding delays due to material 
shortages. 
 
The prefabricated elements, corresponding to the dock modules and support structures, 
were transported to the installation site using flatbed trailers. In this process, it was essential 
to verify that the materials delivered matched the approved design and were in suitable 
condition for immediate on-site assembly. 
 
Additionally, I maintained continuous coordination with the internal team responsible for 
the manufacturing and forging of the structures, which worked with materials such as 
galvanized steel, metal pipes, wooden boards, and fastening elements (screws, bolts, and 
anchors). This interaction allowed fabrication timelines to be aligned with installation 
schedules and ensured that technical specifications were correctly interpreted and executed. 
 
These activities required constant communication with different areas of the company, 
particularly production, logistics, and operations, as well as with external suppliers when 
necessary. Overall, the work performed contributed to improving pre-installation planning, 
reducing errors in material shipments, and strengthening coordination between the 
manufacturing and on-site assembly stages.
 
2.2 Most Challenging Activity 
 
The most challenging activity involved the technical evaluation of a modular dock project 
that required the integration of a hydraulic lift for a vessel with a higher-than-usual weight. 
The main challenge was to determine whether the modular structure could withstand the 
dynamic loads associated with the operation of the lift while remaining stable under site-
specific environmental conditions. In parallel, it was necessary to analyze the compatibility 

of the hydraulic system with the characteristics of the installation site and to integrate 
transportation, installation, and material costs within a limited budget. 
 
To address these challenges, different engineering alternatives were evaluated. Two 
reinforced modular configurations were compared, and load simulations were conducted 
based on both average and maximum operating conditions. In addition, alternative 
anchoring systems were analyzed to improve structural stability. Based on this analysis, a 
reinforced configuration with a mixed anchoring system was recommended, and the project 
budget was adjusted while maintaining overall economic feasibility. Figure 4 illustrates the 
process during the implementation of the reinforced solution, including the assembly and 
adjustment of the modular dock structure. 
 
Figure 4. Adjustment of the modular dock after the implementation of the reinforced conﬁguration and mixed 
anchoring system. 
Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). 
 
One of the main errors made during the evaluation and installation of the project was the 
inadequate consideration of the lakebed characteristics in the designated installation area. 

During the planning phase, the ground was assumed to be relatively firm and 
homogeneous; however, once installation began, it became evident that the substrate was 
sandy and of low density, significantly reducing its load-bearing capacity. 
 
As a result, some sections of the modular dock began to partially sink, compromising the 
leveling and overall stability of the structure. This issue required the team to dismantle the 
affected section and redesign the solution by installing a larger metallic base to distribute 
loads more effectively across the lakebed. With this modification, the modules regained 
stability and the installation was successfully completed. 
 
This experience led to a fundamental learning outcome: the need to systematically assess 
submerged ground conditions, as support points, anchoring systems, and structural 
transitions must account for the bearing capacity of the lakebed. For future projects, it is 
essential to incorporate lakebed inspection protocols and hold preliminary meetings with 
clients to validate environmental and site conditions. This case reinforced the importance of 
prior technical verification, rapid adaptation to unforeseen conditions, and effective 
collaboration between engineering and operations to implement safe and efficient solutions.  
3. Analysis 
3.1 Company Analysis 
Smart Dock operates with a highly efficient and flexible production and installation model, 
based on prefabrication and modular systems, which significantly enhances productivity 
and operational capacity. 
 

From a logistics and production perspective, at the beginning of each month the company 
receives a shipment containing the base structures and key components required for 
approximately ten different projects. These shipments include galvanized steel components, 
hydraulic motors, battery systems, solar charging panels, wooden bases, and structural 
accessories. This monthly procurement strategy allows Smart Dock to plan production 
activities in advance and secure materials for the following month’s operations, reducing 
supply risks and downtime. 
 
The production and assembly team focuses on pre-assembling, welding, and galvanizing 
metallic structures corresponding to the projects scheduled for the upcoming month. This 
prefabrication process enables most structural elements to arrive at the installation site 
already prepared, limiting on-site work to final assembly and adjustments. As a result, 
installation times are significantly reduced. 
 
In terms of productivity, Smart Dock completes on average approximately ten projects per 
month, with individual projects typically finalized within a single day. Due to the modular 
and prefabricated nature of the systems, the company has demonstrated the ability to 
complete up to two projects in one day, achieving this level of productivity on multiple 
occasions. During my internship period, I participated in around 20 projects across different 
lakes in southern Chile, reflecting a high operational workload. Figures 5 and 6 present 
examples of completed modular dock projects installed during this period, illustrating the 
scale, diversity, and final quality of the implemented solutions. 

 
Figure 5. Installed hydraulic lift system, showing the ﬁnal conﬁguration and structural support after on-site 
installation. 
Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration) 
 
Figure 6. Completed nautical infrastructure project combining a modular dock system and a hydraulic lift, 
illustrating the integrated solution delivered to the client. 
Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration) 

On an annual basis, Smart Dock executes approximately 40 projects per year. With the 
ability to scale up during peak demand periods. Notably, there is a strong seasonal demand 
between December and February, extending into March, when clients typically prepare for 
summer use. During this period, the company completes more than 30 projects, while the 
remaining months of the year account for fewer than 10 projects. This seasonality requires 
flexible allocation of labor and production resources. 
 
Regarding human resources, Smart Dock employs more than 20 workers, distributed across 
management, production, logistics, supply chain, and field operations. Due to the 
company’s size and project-based structure, tasks are often shared across areas, promoting 
versatility among employees and facilitating coordination between production and 
installation teams. 
 
Overall, the combination of advance material planning, prefabrication, and modular design 
allows Smart Dock to maintain high productivity levels, rapid project execution, and adapt 
to seasonal demand, positioning the company as an efficient and competitive provider of 
modular nautical infrastructure. 
 
3.2 Work Analysis 
My work contributed to the organization across multiple areas, particularly through 
technical and operational support to project execution. From a technical perspective, I 
participated in the comparative evaluation of different modular dock alternatives, which 
supported decision-making processes regarding structural configurations and installation 
feasibility. Additionally, I contributed to improving material traceability by supporting 
more systematic tracking of components throughout the preparation and installation stages. 
 

From an operational standpoint, my work involved identifying logistical bottlenecks that 
affected material availability and installation timelines. These observations allowed for the 
proposal of improvements to the supply and procurement processes, with the objective of 
reducing delays and increasing operational efficiency. 
 
During the internship, certain errors and improvement opportunities were identified, mainly 
related to planning and coordination between areas. These experiences highlighted the need 
for more structured procedures. As a result, several improvement measures were proposed, 
including the implementation of standardized installation checklists to reduce execution 
errors, the standardization of cost sheets to improve budgeting accuracy, and stronger 
integration between the logistics and engineering areas to enhance coordination and 
information flow. 
 
Overall, the work performed contributed to process improvements, supported operational 
efficiency, and provided valuable insights for strengthening internal coordination within the 
company.
 
4. Personal Reflection 
 
The internship at Smart Dock was a highly enriching experience, both professionally and 
personally. I was able to apply engineering knowledge in a real-world context, developing 
skills in technical evaluation, logistics management, and client communication. On a 
personal level, I learned to work under pressure, collaborate with multidisciplinary teams, 
and adapt to changing environments. This experience confirmed my interest in the logistics 
area and reinforced my motivation to continue developing my career in applied 
engineering.

competencias_activas.nombre: Utiliza conocimientos y metodologías de ciencias básicas y ciencias de la ingeniería
competencias_activas.descripcion: Utiliza conocimientos y metodologías de ciencias básicas y ciencias de la ingeniería, formulando problemas de ingeniería que combinan la teoría y la práctica en su solución.

competencias_activas.nombre: Aplica las técnicas y métodos de las ciencias de la ingeniería a la resolución de problemas fundamentales
competencias_activas.descripcion: Aplica las técnicas y métodos de las ciencias de la ingeniería a la resolución de problemas fundamentales, utilizando nuevas tecnologías y tomando en cuenta los supuestos y limitaciones teóricas y prácticas.
Integra eficazmente equipos de trabajo, estableciendo metas, planificando tareas y cumpliendo objetivos en los plazos requeridos.

competencias_activas.nombre: Se comunica por escrito y oralmente de forma efectiva tanto en español como en inglés
competencias_activas.descripcion: Se comunica por escrito y oralmente de forma efectiva tanto en español como en inglés, especialmente en temas relativos a su profesión.

This interaction allowed fabrication timelines to be aligned with installation  schedules and ensured that technical specifications were correctly interpreted and executed. These activities required constant communication with different areas of the company,  particularly production, logistics, and operations, as well as with external suppliers when  necessary.

Overall, the work performed contributed to improving pre-installation planning,  reducing errors in material shipments, and strengthening coordination between the  manufacturing and on-site assembly stages.

2.2 Most Challenging Activity    The most challenging activity involved the technical evaluation of a modular dock project  that required the integration of a hydraulic lift for a vessel with a higher-than-usual weight. The main challenge was to determine whether the modular structure could withstand the  dynamic loads associated with the operation of the lift while remaining stable under site- specific environmental conditions.

In parallel, it was necessary to analyze the compatibility   of the hydraulic system with the characteristics of the installation site and to integrate  transportation, installation, and material costs within a limited budget. To address these challenges, different engineering alternatives were evaluated. Two  reinforced modular configurations were compared, and load simulations were conducted  based on both average and maximum operating conditions.

In addition, alternative  anchoring systems were analyzed to improve structural stability. Based on this analysis, a  reinforced configuration with a mixed anchoring system was recommended, and the project  budget was adjusted while maintaining overall economic feasibility. Figure 4 illustrates the  process during the implementation of the reinforced solution, including the assembly and  adjustment of the modular dock structure. Figure 4.

Adjustment of the modular dock after the implementation of the reinforced conﬁguration and mixed  anchoring system. Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). One of the main errors made during the evaluation and installation of the project was the  inadequate consideration of the lakebed characteristics in the designated installation area.

During the planning phase, the ground was assumed to be relatively firm and  homogeneous; however, once installation began, it became evident that the substrate was  sandy and of low density, significantly reducing its load-bearing capacity. As a result, some sections of the modular dock began to partially sink, compromising the  leveling and overall stability of the structure.

This issue required the team to dismantle the  affected section and redesign the solution by installing a larger metallic base to distribute  loads more effectively across the lakebed. With this modification, the modules regained  stability and the installation was successfully completed.

This experience led to a fundamental learning outcome: the need to systematically assess  submerged ground conditions, as support points, anchoring systems, and structural  transitions must account for the bearing capacity of the lakebed. For future projects, it is  essential to incorporate lakebed inspection protocols and hold preliminary meetings with  clients to validate environmental and site conditions.

This case reinforced the importance of  prior technical verification, rapid adaptation to unforeseen conditions, and effective  collaboration between engineering and operations to implement safe and efficient solutions. 3. Analysis.

3.1 Company Analysis  Smart Dock operates with a highly efficient and flexible production and installation model,  based on prefabrication and modular systems, which significantly enhances productivity  and operational capacity. From a logistics and production perspective, at the beginning of each month the company  receives a shipment containing the base structures and key components required for  approximately ten different projects.

These shipments include galvanized steel components,  hydraulic motors, battery systems, solar charging panels, wooden bases, and structural  accessories. This monthly procurement strategy allows Smart Dock to plan production  activities in advance and secure materials for the following month’s operations, reducing  supply risks and downtime.

The production and assembly team focuses on pre-assembling, welding, and galvanizing  metallic structures corresponding to the projects scheduled for the upcoming month. This  prefabrication process enables most structural elements to arrive at the installation site  already prepared, limiting on-site work to final assembly and adjustments. As a result,  installation times are significantly reduced.

In terms of productivity, Smart Dock completes on average approximately ten projects per  month, with individual projects typically finalized within a single day. Due to the modular  and prefabricated nature of the systems, the company has demonstrated the ability to  complete up to two projects in one day, achieving this level of productivity on multiple  occasions.

During my internship period, I participated in around 20 projects across different  lakes in southern Chile, reflecting a high operational workload. Figures 5 and 6 present  examples of completed modular dock projects installed during this period, illustrating the  scale, diversity, and final quality of the implemented solutions. Figure 5. Installed hydraulic lift system, showing the ﬁnal conﬁguration and structural support after on-site  installation.

Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration)    Figure 6. Completed nautical infrastructure project combining a modular dock system and a hydraulic lift,  illustrating the integrated solution delivered to the client. Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration)   On an annual basis, Smart Dock executes approximately 40 projects per year. With the  ability to scale up during peak demand periods.

Notably, there is a strong seasonal demand  between December and February, extending into March, when clients typically prepare for  summer use. During this period, the company completes more than 30 projects, while the  remaining months of the year account for fewer than 10 projects. This seasonality requires  flexible allocation of labor and production resources.

Regarding human resources, Smart Dock employs more than 20 workers, distributed across  management, production, logistics, supply chain, and field operations. Due to the  company’s size and project-based structure, tasks are often shared across areas, promoting  versatility among employees and facilitating coordination between production and  installation teams.

Overall, the combination of advance material planning, prefabrication, and modular design  allows Smart Dock to maintain high productivity levels, rapid project execution, and adapt  to seasonal demand, positioning the company as an efficient and competitive provider of  modular nautical infrastructure.

3.2 Work Analysis  My work contributed to the organization across multiple areas, particularly through  technical and operational support to project execution. From a technical perspective, I  participated in the comparative evaluation of different modular dock alternatives, which  supported decision-making processes regarding structural configurations and installation  feasibility.

Additionally, I contributed to improving material traceability by supporting  more systematic tracking of components throughout the preparation and installation stages. From an operational standpoint, my work involved identifying logistical bottlenecks that  affected material availability and installation timelines. These observations allowed for the  proposal of improvements to the supply and procurement processes, with the objective of  reducing delays and increasing operational efficiency.

During the internship, certain errors and improvement opportunities were identified, mainly  related to planning and coordination between areas. These experiences highlighted the need  for more structured procedures.

As a result, several improvement measures were proposed,  including the implementation of standardized installation checklists to reduce execution  errors, the standardization of cost sheets to improve budgeting accuracy, and stronger  integration between the logistics and engineering areas to enhance coordination and  information flow.
Overall, the work performed contributed to process improvements, supported operational  efficiency, and provided valuable insights for strengthening internal coordination within the  company. 4.

Personal Reflection    The internship at Smart Dock was a highly enriching experience, both professionally and  personally. I was able to apply engineering knowledge in a real-world context, developing  skills in technical evaluation, logistics management, and client communication. On a  personal level, I learned to work under pressure, collaborate with multidisciplinary teams,  and adapt to changing environments.
This experience confirmed my interest in the logistics  area and reinforced my motivation to continue developing my career in applied  engineering.

embeddings_data.texto: Abstract  Smart Dock is a company dedicated to the design and production of modular docks and  hydraulic lifts for boats and jet skis. During my pre-professional practice, I participated in  activities related to material logistics for the installation of docks and lifts, technical and  economic project analysis, and direct interaction with clients. Among the most relevant  tasks were the planning and coordination of modular structure installations.

As part of this  planning process, we requested photographs from clients showing the desired installation  area, along with information regarding lakebed conditions, in order to evaluate alternative  assembly and installation solutions. The main challenge consisted of integrating technical and logistical criteria in projects with  time and environmental constraints, where installation planning was critical.

This was  particularly relevant given that docks and hydraulic lifts were installed across several lakes  in southern Chile, while construction and installation materials were stored in Cunco,  Araucanía. Each project had to be completed within a single day, and for jet ski lifts it was  possible to complete two different projects in one day.

The results of my internship were reflected in the significant development of my skills in  operational management, engineering analysis, and professional communication,  strengthening my training and my ability to adapt to multidisciplinary environments. 1. Introduction.

1.1 Company Description  Smart Dock is a Chilean company dedicated to the design, manufacturing, and installation  of nautical infrastructure solutions, specializing in modular docks and hydraulic lifts for  boats and jet skis. The company began its operations in 2019, initially focusing on the  installation of hydraulic lift systems. Figure 1 presents an example of a hydraulic lift  system installed by the company. Figure 1. Hydraulic lift system for boats and jet skis installed by Smart Dock.

Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). Starting in 2020, Smart Dock incorporated modular dock systems. These systems have been  widely implemented in lakes across southern Chile, as illustrated in Figure 2, enabling  Smart Dock to reduce installation times and operate efficiently. Figure 2. Modular dock system installed in a lake in southern Chile. Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). Since its foundation, the company has developed more than 200 projects across various  lakes in southern Chile.

On average, Smart Dock executes more than 40 projects per year,  maintaining a high project turnover and an operational capacity that allows it to manage  multiple installations simultaneously. Based on the scale and volume of its operations, the  company generates estimated annual revenues of 400 million Chilean pesos, demonstrating  sustainable growth and operational stability.

Organizational Chart    The company has approximately 20 employees and operates under an integrated model that  combines engineering, manufacturing and installation. This structure, which facilitates  coordination and decision-making, is presented in Figure 3. Figure 3. Organizational chart of Smart Dock. Source: Own elaboration. 1.2 Department /.

Area Description  My professional internship was carried out mainly within the Project Execution area, which  is responsible for planning, coordinating, and executing the installation of modular docks  and hydraulic lift systems. This area plays a key role in connecting engineering design,  production, logistics, and on-site installation activities.

The main responsibilities of the Operations and Project Execution area include material  planning for each project, coordination with the production team, scheduling of  installations, and supervision of on-site execution. Due to the modular and prefabricated  nature of the company’s solutions, the area must ensure that all components are properly  prepared prior to transportation in order to meet strict installation timelines.

Within this area, I worked directly under the supervision of the Project Manager – Field  Operations, maintaining close interaction with the production and logistics teams. My  responsibilities included supporting the planning and preparation of materials, coordinating   with the production team to align fabrication and delivery schedules, and assisting in on- site installation activities.

I also participated in the technical and economic evaluation of  projects and supported communication with clients to gather relevant information for  installation planning. The Operations and Project Execution area maintains constant interaction with other  departments, particularly Production and Supply Chain, to ensure material availability and  quality.

This coordination is essential for completing projects efficiently, often within a  single day, and for adapting installation strategies to site-specific conditions. Overall, this area provided a multidisciplinary working environment that allowed me to  apply engineering concepts in real projects while developing operational, organizational,  and communication skills.

My supervisor was Agustín Molina García, who was responsible  for coordinating projects from their design phase through to installation. The working  relationship was close and focused on continuous feedback, which allowed me to develop  both technical and operational criteria. 2. Work Details.

2.1 General Activities  During the internship period, I carried out various activities aimed at supporting the  execution of Smart Dock’s nautical infrastructure projects, with an emphasis on logistics  planning, internal coordination, and on-site installation support. One of my main responsibilities was the planning and preparation of the materials required  for each project, considering the technical specifications and the specific configuration of   each modular dock or hydraulic lift system.

This planning involved defining in advance the  quantity and type of modules, metal structures, and auxiliary components required, with the  objective of ensuring continuity during installation and avoiding delays due to material  shortages. The prefabricated elements, corresponding to the dock modules and support structures,  were transported to the installation site using flatbed trailers.

In this process, it was essential  to verify that the materials delivered matched the approved design and were in suitable  condition for immediate on-site assembly. Additionally, I maintained continuous coordination with the internal team responsible for  the manufacturing and forging of the structures, which worked with materials such as  galvanized steel, metal pipes, wooden boards, and fastening elements (screws, bolts, and  anchors).

This interaction allowed fabrication timelines to be aligned with installation  schedules and ensured that technical specifications were correctly interpreted and executed. These activities required constant communication with different areas of the company,  particularly production, logistics, and operations, as well as with external suppliers when  necessary.

Overall, the work performed contributed to improving pre-installation planning,  reducing errors in material shipments, and strengthening coordination between the  manufacturing and on-site assembly stages.

2.2 Most Challenging Activity    The most challenging activity involved the technical evaluation of a modular dock project  that required the integration of a hydraulic lift for a vessel with a higher-than-usual weight. The main challenge was to determine whether the modular structure could withstand the  dynamic loads associated with the operation of the lift while remaining stable under site- specific environmental conditions.

In parallel, it was necessary to analyze the compatibility   of the hydraulic system with the characteristics of the installation site and to integrate  transportation, installation, and material costs within a limited budget. To address these challenges, different engineering alternatives were evaluated. Two  reinforced modular configurations were compared, and load simulations were conducted  based on both average and maximum operating conditions.

In addition, alternative  anchoring systems were analyzed to improve structural stability. Based on this analysis, a  reinforced configuration with a mixed anchoring system was recommended, and the project  budget was adjusted while maintaining overall economic feasibility. Figure 4 illustrates the  process during the implementation of the reinforced solution, including the assembly and  adjustment of the modular dock structure. Figure 4.

Adjustment of the modular dock after the implementation of the reinforced conﬁguration and mixed  anchoring system. Source: Smart Dock (own elaboration). During the planning phase, the ground was assumed to be relatively firm and  homogeneous; however, once installation began, it became evident that the substrate was  sandy and of low density, significantly reducing its load-bearing capacity. As a result, some sections of the modular dock began to partially sink, compromising the  leveling and overall stability of the structure.

This issue required the team to dismantle the  affected section and redesign the solution by installing a larger metallic base to distribute  loads more effectively across the lakebed. With this modification, the modules regained  stability and the installation was successfully completed.

This experience led to a fundamental learning outcome: the need to systematically assess  submerged ground conditions, as support points, anchoring systems, and structural  transitions must account for the bearing capacity of the lakebed.
